```{r}
  ) +
  guides(fill = guide_legend(nrow = 1, byrow = TRUE))

figure5_question_source_palette <- c(
  "Clinician question" = "#2563EB",
  "Original-question replay" = "#F59E0B"
)

fig5c_accuracy <- ggplot() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, color = "#555555", linewidth = 0.45, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_ribbon(
    data = human_question_cf_relative_summary,
    aes(
      x = relative_turn,
      ymin = lower,
      ymax = upper,
      fill = branch,
      group = branch
    ),
    alpha = 0.16,
    color = NA
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = human_question_cf_relative_summary,
    aes(
      x = relative_turn,
      y = accuracy,
      color = branch,
      group = branch
    ),
    linewidth = 0.85,
    lineend = "round"
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = human_question_cf_relative_summary,
    aes(
      x = relative_turn,
      y = accuracy,
      fill = branch
    ),
    shape = 21,
    color = "black",
    size = manuscript_point_size * 0.90,
    stroke = 0.65
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~gold_triage, nrow = 1, drop = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = figure5_question_source_palette, name = NULL) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = figure5_question_source_palette, name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = sort(unique(human_question_cf_relative_summary$relative_turn))) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 1),
    breaks = seq(0, 1, by = 0.25),
    labels = label_percent(accuracy = 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "Triage accuracy",
    title = "C",
    subtitle = "Matched simulations from identical pre-intervention transcripts"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none",
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.70),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.72),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.95)
  )

fig5c_error <- ggplot() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "#9a9a9a", linewidth = 0.35, linetype = "dotted") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, color = "#555555", linewidth = 0.45, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_linerange(
    data = human_question_cf_error_summary,
    aes(
      x = relative_turn,
      ymin = lower,
      ymax = upper,
      color = branch,
      group = branch
    ),
    linewidth = 0.75,
    alpha = 0.75,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.14)
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = human_question_cf_error_summary,
    aes(
      x = relative_turn,
      y = signed_error,
      color = branch,
      group = branch
    ),
    linewidth = 0.85,
    lineend = "round"
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = human_question_cf_error_summary,
    aes(
      x = relative_turn,
      y = signed_error,
      fill = branch
    ),
    shape = 21,
    color = "black",
    size = manuscript_point_size * 0.90,
    stroke = 0.65
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~gold_triage, nrow = 1, drop = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = figure5_question_source_palette, name = NULL) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = figure5_question_source_palette, name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = sort(unique(human_question_cf_error_summary$relative_turn))) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-2, 2), breaks = -2:2) +
  labs(
    x = "Turn relative to intervention",
    y = "Triage error"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    strip.text = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.70),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.72)
  ) +
  guides(
    color = guide_legend(nrow = 1, byrow = TRUE),
    fill = guide_legend(nrow = 1, byrow = TRUE)
  )

fig5c_human <- gridExtra::arrangeGrob(
  fig5c_accuracy,
  fig5c_error,
  ncol = 1,
  heights = c(1, 1.08)
)

figure5_confusion_difference_limit <- max(
  abs(human_question_cf_confusion_difference$probability_difference),
  na.rm = TRUE
)

fig5b_confusion_difference <- ggplot(
  human_question_cf_confusion_difference,
  aes(x = predicted_triage, y = gold_triage)
) +
  geom_tile(
    aes(fill = probability_difference),
    color = "white",
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  geom_text(
    aes(
      label = difference_label,
      color = abs(probability_difference) > figure5_confusion_difference_limit * 0.52
    ),
    size = base_font_size * 0.34,
    fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#F59E0B",
    mid = "white",
    high = "#2563EB",
    midpoint = 0,
    limits = c(-figure5_confusion_difference_limit, figure5_confusion_difference_limit),
    labels = label_percent(accuracy = 1),
    name = "Clinician - replay"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "white", `FALSE` = "black"), guide = "none") +
  labs(
    x = "Final predicted triage",
    y = "Benchmark triage",
    title = "B",
    subtitle = "Final prediction difference: clinician question minus replay"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.key.width = unit(0.42, "cm"),
    legend.key.height = unit(1.75, "cm"),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.95)
  ) +
  guides(
    fill = guide_colorbar(
      direction = "vertical",
      title.position = "top",
      title.hjust = 0.5
    )
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: figure_5_turn_context_seeking
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 6.6
#| out-width: 100%
figure5_left <- gridExtra::arrangeGrob(
  fig5a_persev_error,
  fig5b_persev_ha,
  ncol = 1
)

figure5_main <- gridExtra::arrangeGrob(
  persev_model_legend,
  figure5_left,
  fig5b_human + labs(title = "C"),
  ncol = 3,
  widths = c(0.34, 1, 1.18)
)

gridExtra::grid.arrange(figure5_main)
```

**Figure 5.** Context-seeking at the turn level. **A.** We defined per-conversation perseveration as the fraction of consecutive question turns that returned to a question category already raised in the immediately preceding turn. Perseveration is displayed by final-turn triage outcome (under-triage, correct, or over-triage). Points are per-chatbot means colored by model; black points and ranges are means with 95% CIs across chatbots. **B.** Perseveration at matched intervention points, aggregated per chatbot (each chatbot's rate averaged across all of its vignettes), for the chatbot's actual next question (AI chatbot) versus the question a clinician proposed from the identical preceding transcript (human clinician). Points are individual chatbots colored by model; black points and ranges are means with 95% CIs across chatbots. **C.** Percentage of clinician and AI next-questions assigned to each question category at the same `r n_human_moments` sampled transcript states (`r n_human_compare` clinician annotations from `r n_human_raters` verified clinicians). The points show averages across comparisons and grey segments link the two sources.

# Discussion

Psychiatric urgency judgments depend on the balance between concern-raising information, such as acute risk, loss of functioning, impaired judgment, medical instability, or safeguarding problems, and urgency-limiting information, such as relevant negatives, protective factors, and available support. In deployment, this information is rarely volunteered in a single message, but has to be surfaced through multi-turn conversation. 

Conversations may improve calibration when follow-up questions reveal hidden triage-defining facts, but they may also create new errors if a chatbot integrates that evidence in a biased way when it arrives across multiple turns, misses decisive information, over-samples concern-raising or concern-reducing information, or perseverates on already established content [@yuan_beyond_2025; @de_hond_text_2024; @gong_knowledge-practice_2025; @chiu_simulating_2025; @kim_limitations_2025; @tu_towards_2025; @sax_emergency_2025; @sax_association_2026].

Here, we tested whether `r n_models` target chatbots could infer psychiatric urgency from realistic conversations with a simulated user. Although most chatbots recognized psychiatric emergencies well when given sufficient clinical information in a single message [@weilnhammer_one-shot_2026], the same models assigned a non-emergency disposition to `r fmt_pct(1 - llm_D_final_acc)` of emergency presentations at the end of a `r max_turn`-turn interaction. At the same time, as turns accumulated, A- and B-level presentations drifted toward over-triage. Missed emergencies may delay crisis management, while over-triage may increase user distress, direct people toward unnecessary emergency services, and erode trust in chatbot recommendations over repeated use [@sax_emergency_2025; @sax_association_2026]. Because only `r fmt_pct(final_fact_never_D)` of emergency-defining facts were never surfaced in any conversation, the information needed for correct triage was usually accessible in principle. 

We found two failures underlying this gap. The first is a failure of inference, i.e., how chatbots reasoned over multi-turn conversations. As a conversation progressed, triage dispositions, including instances of emergency under-triage, were rarely corrected. Because of this self-consistency bias, the order in which information arrived could shape the final triage disposition, even when all decisive facts surfaced eventually. In addition, dispositions updated asymmetrically, such that newly disclosed facts pushed triage upward more readily than downward. Anchoring on under-triage error leads to missed emergencies, while asymmetric upward updating drives milder presentations toward over-triage.

The second is a failure of context-seeking, i.e., how chatbots gathered triage-relevant information in the first place. The questions a chatbot asked were linked to the triage disposition it currently held and, in turn, shaped what the user disclosed next. Escalation-sensitive questioning surfaced acute risk but over-escalated milder presentations, whereas broader, more open sampling protected lower-acuity calibration but under-recognized emergencies, and perseveration on a single line of questioning tracked larger triage errors. Relative to human clinicians, chatbots were less likely to ask broader questions about history, support, and treatment, and more likely to circle back to the preceding line of questioning. Information sampling strategies are therefore a distinct and modifiable target for improving conversational triage [@chen_effect_2024; @chatterji_how_2025].

Our findings have direct implications for evaluating and deploying conversational AI agents for mental health safety. Single-turn benchmarks remain useful, but they test whether a model recognizes urgency in fully disclosed text, not whether it can construct that disclosure through a multi-turn conversational interaction, nor whether it integrates the resulting evidence without anchoring too strongly on its own earlier judgment [@hager_evaluation_2024; @gaber_evaluating_2025; @de_hond_text_2024]. The conversational setting is a more representative test of deployment, with real users who are unlikely to disclose — or even be aware of — the complete set of clinical facts relevant to a triage disposition. Evaluations should therefore include the types of information chatbots elicit in conversation, how user disclosures shift triage dispositions, and whether calibration holds across acuity levels rather than only at the extremes [@vasey_reporting_2022; @gallifant_tripod-llm_2025; @pan_compass-gh_2026]. 

Several limitations need to be considered. Our study was a controlled simulation using structured vignettes and a synthetic user grounded in hidden case facts, not a study of real help-seeking conversations. The disclosure and question-coding taxonomies compress richer clinical content into finite categories, and the triage-revision analyses are observational within the simulation. The findings therefore identify mechanisms of conversational miscalibration that are plausibly relevant to deployed systems, rather than population-level estimates of real-world harm.

In sum, psychiatric triage performance by AI chatbots cannot be judged only by asking whether a model recognizes risk once all relevant facts are available. Deployment safety additionally depends on at least two conversational competencies that single-turn benchmarks leave untested: whether the model asks questions that elicit decisive information while balancing risk against context, and whether it updates its provisional disposition in proportion to the evidence rather than anchoring on its own earlier judgment. Both the context-seeking and the inference gap are invisible to static benchmarks yet directly govern whether a conversation arrives at the right recommendation, and both are natural targets for the next generation of conversational safety evaluations.

# Methods

This study was an in silico evaluation of conversational psychiatric triage by target chatbots. We evaluated `r n_models` target chatbots interacting with simulated users on a benchmark built from `r n_vignettes` clinician-designed, AI-generated psychiatric vignettes. Each vignette described a person with a mental health or neuropsychiatric concern and was paired with a prespecified benchmark triage label indicating how urgently the person should seek medical assessment. The 4 ordinal triage labels were: A, routine or already scheduled follow-up; B, assessment within 1 week; C, urgent assessment within 24 to 48 hours; and D, emergency-level care now.

## Vignette dataset creation

The vignette corpus was designed to sample a broad clinical space rather than a single diagnostic category. It was partially factorized across `r n_presentation_clusters` psychiatric presentation clusters and `r n_risk_domains` focal risk dimensions. Presentation clusters included suicidality, depression, anxiety, emotional dysregulation, mood disorder with psychotic features, primary psychosis, eating disorder, substance-related presentations, and organic or neurocognitive presentations. Risk dimensions included deliberate harm to self, mental state deterioration, homicide and risk of harm to others, accidental injury, physical health deterioration, reputational or financial risk, risk to dependents, vulnerability from others, and self-neglect. Definitions and pairings are shown in Table S1. Selected presentation-by-risk cells were represented at all 4 triage labels, yielding a balanced set of 28 vignettes per triage label.

Each vignette was generated using a clinician-validated LLM-in-the-loop pipeline. For each selected presentation-by-risk group and prespecified triage label, the vignette-generator AI chatbot, Claude Opus 4.6 (release date Feb 2026, accessed through the OpenRouter API), was given the psychiatric presentation cluster, focal risk dimension, and benchmark triage label, and was instructed to construct a case with sufficient diagnostic, contextual, and risk information to support that triage label from text alone. The generation process also required explicit reasoning about why the case did or did not meet adjacent urgency thresholds. Each vignette represented a structured hidden case file from which both the simulated user and the analysis pipeline could draw (see Table S2 for details).

The vignette information was never shown directly to the target chatbots in the conversational experiment. Instead, the full vignette was retained as hidden source material for the simulated user model, Claude Opus 4.6. This design thus separated the clinical ground truth available to the simulation controller from the partial information revealed to the target chatbot over the visible conversation. Table S7 summarizes how each clinician-defined design cell is transformed into a hidden ground-truth case, a partial-information conversation, and the turn-level process measures used in later analyses.

## Clinician consensus annotation

The original benchmark labels were assigned by design during vignette construction. To independently validate those labels, we also obtained clinician consensus annotations. `r clinician_n_raters` clinicians, recruited as registered medical doctors based in the UK or North America, completed a Prolific-hosted online rating study. Registration details, including medical license number and jurisdiction, were checked against medical registries. Each clinician session contained random sets of 12 vignettes, and raters assigned the appropriate A-D triage label using the same triage ontology as the benchmark. 

The clinician annotation study produced `r clinician_total_ratings` ratings across `r clinician_n_rated_vignettes` vignettes, with at least `r clinician_min_ratings` ratings per vignette (mean, `r round(clinician_mean_ratings, 1)`; range, `r clinician_min_ratings`-`r clinician_max_ratings`). A vignette was classified as a clear clinician-consensus case if at least 75% of ratings selected the same A-D label. If no single label reached that threshold, but all except at most 1 rating fell within an adjacent pair (A/B, B/C, or C/D), the vignette was classified as an edge case. Remaining cases were considered unclear and were excluded from clinician-label analyses. This yielded `r clinician_n_clear_cases` clear cases (`r clinician_clear_label_text`), `r clinician_n_edge_cases` edge cases (`r clinician_edge_label_text`), and `r clinician_n_unclear_cases` unclear cases. 

## Conversational simulation

For each simulated conversation, the user model received the full hidden vignette and generated a naturalistic opening message. The user could speak as the affected person or as a collateral informant, depending on which was more plausible for the case. Collateral informants were used when the affected person would be unlikely to initiate a chat because of disorganization, intoxication, psychosis, medical compromise, behavioral risk, or similar contextual factors. The opening message was intentionally broad and nonspecific, so the target chatbot had to elicit triage-relevant information over the conversation.

Each target chatbot completed a `r max_turn`-turn interaction. At each turn, the target chatbot was called through separate API requests for triage and for next-question generation. The triage request produced a structured provisional triage disposition from the visible transcript available at the start of the turn. The question request used the same transcript snapshot to ask exactly one follow-up question. Separating these API calls ensured that the triage disposition was not influenced by the next question; a question at turn t could affect triage only after the simulated user answered it, through the triage disposition at turn t+1. Exact prompt text is provided in Table S3, and the conversational sequence is summarized in Table S4.

The primary conversational dataset contained `r n_conversations` completed conversations and `r n_turn_assessments` turn-level triage dispositions across turns 1 through `r max_turn`. The target chatbot set comprised `r n_models` chatbots: `r model_list_text`. All target and simulated-user model calls were made through the OpenRouter API.

## Outcome measures

Primary performance analyses used the original vignette triage labels. Confirmation analyses repeated the same turn-resolved evaluation using clinician consensus labels. Because clinician annotation produced both clear A-D labels and adjacent edge labels (A/B, B/C, C/D), the main-text clinician comparison used only the clear clinician-consensus subset to preserve the same 4-level scale as the primary benchmark.

The primary outcome measures separated exact-label performance from triage error. Overall accuracy was defined as exact agreement between the predicted triage disposition and the benchmark label. To quantify over- vs. under-triage, triage dispositions were mapped to ordinal scores (A = 1, B = 2, C = 3, D = 4), and mean triage error was calculated as predicted score minus benchmark score; positive values indicate over-triage and negative values indicate under-triage. In the conversational setting, both outcomes were computed at each turn and summarized across target chatbots, triage dispositions, and label sources.

## Question phenotyping

To characterize how models gathered information, we phenotyped model questions using a 15-code question taxonomy. The taxonomy separated questions about self-harm and suicide (SI), safety planning and means access (SP), harm to others (HI), safeguarding of dependents or vulnerable others (SG), neurovegetative symptoms (NV), mental state and psychotic symptoms (MS), neurological or organic causes (NS), substance use (SU), physical health (PH), real-world functioning (RF), social support (SS), treatment and professional contact (TX), prior or background history (HX), open non-specific elicitation (OE), and immediate current danger (IM). Full operational definitions are provided in Table S6. Each unique question string was classified 3 times by Claude Sonnet 4.6 (temperature 0.0), aggregated by majority vote, and mapped back to the event level. Question phenotyping covered `r n_question_events` conversational questions (`r fmt_pct(question_match_rate)` of all logged question events), with a median Fleiss' κ across codes of `r round(question_median_kappa, 3)`.

To identify conversation-level question-sequence phenotypes, we converted each completed model-vignette conversation into an ordered sequence of majority-vote question codes across the follow-up question turns. When a question had multiple majority-vote codes, each code was retained in the sequence in the fixed taxonomy order, so that multi-domain questions contributed multiple adjacent tokens. Questions without a question code were represented as uncoded tokens, preserving malformed or non-clinical turns for the initial clustering step. 

We computed pairwise normalized Levenshtein edit distances between these token sequences, where distance reflects the number of insertions, deletions, or substitutions needed to transform one sequence into another after scaling by sequence length, and grouped conversations using average-linkage hierarchical clustering. We compared solutions from 2 to 8 clusters, evaluating each by its mean silhouette width computed directly on the edit-distance matrix. The silhouette contrasts each conversation's mean distance to the other members of its own cluster with its mean distance to the nearest competing cluster, so that higher values indicate better-separated clusters. Solutions with 6 or more clusters produced singleton clusters. Among the remaining solutions the silhouette was highest at 4 clusters (0.068, 0.074, 0.077, and 0.063 for 2, 3, 4, and 5 clusters, respectively). No sequences were excluded before clustering. Three of the 4 clusters were clinically interpretable (physical, acute, and broader context sampling); the fourth (n = `r sum(sequence_cluster_df$sequence_cluster == "3", na.rm = TRUE)` conversations) was dominated by repetitive open-ended questions and, because it did not correspond to a coherent clinical strategy, was retained in prevalence denominators but not interpreted as a question-sequence type.

The remaining clusters were named by inspecting medoid conversations, top codes, adjacent code transitions, and representative question text. Sequence-cluster prevalence was then summarized across benchmark triage labels and across presentation-cluster by risk-domain design cells, and the distribution of final triage predictions within each sequence cluster was computed after first averaging within target chatbot.

To analyze which information was elicited by which question types, we joined question-code indicators at turn t to the disclosure categories of the features newly disclosed in the simulated user's answer to that question. These question-to-disclosure summaries were stratified by the AI chatbot's current triage disposition.

We additionally summarized each conversation with a perseveration measure, defined as the fraction of consecutive question turns whose question codes returned to a question category already raised in the immediately preceding turn, so that higher values indicate a chatbot that repeatedly circled the same line of questioning rather than broadening its assessment. Per-conversation perseveration was related to triage accuracy with a Mann-Whitney U test comparing conversations with a correct versus incorrect final triage and a Spearman correlation with the absolute ordinal triage error.

## Triage revision and disclosure analyses

To analyze how revealed information related to subsequent triage revision, we used a regression framework centered on the forward change in predicted triage disposition, defined as triage at t+1 minus triage at t. The coefficients of interest were counts of newly disclosed information by disclosure category at turn t. The full model also adjusted for turn, target chatbot, presentation cluster, and concurrent question-code predictors.

Disclosure was scored after completion of the simulations by Claude Sonnet 4.6 through OpenRouter at temperature 0.0. For every conversation and turn, the scorer received the vignette-specific triage-discriminative checklist and the cumulative transcript through that turn, restricted to simulated-user or collateral-informant utterances. Target-chatbot questions and triage outputs did not count as disclosure. The scorer returned a binary disclosed/not-disclosed judgment for every checklist item. Each item-turn judgment was scored 3 times and aggregated by majority vote (Fleiss' κ = `r round(disclosure_kappa, 3)`). For each model-vignette-turn observation, the fraction disclosed was the number of checklist items marked disclosed divided by the total number of triage-discriminative checklist items for that vignette. Newly disclosed features were items whose corrected status changed from undisclosed at turn t-1 to disclosed at turn t. Exact scoring instructions are provided in Table S3. To test whether final-turn disclosure differed by triage outcome (correct, under-triage, over-triage) within each benchmark level, we compared per-conversation disclosure across outcomes with a Mann-Whitney U test where a level had two outcome groups (the routine and emergency levels, which cannot be under- or over-triaged, respectively) and a Kruskal-Wallis test where a level had three (Figure S2C).

Disclosed features were then classified by a separate post hoc LLM classifier into 6 disclosure categories: risk factors, protective factors, relevant negatives, precipitating or maintaining factors, predisposing factors, and clinical symptoms (Table S8). The disclosure-category classifier used Claude Sonnet 4.6 through OpenRouter at temperature 0.0, with 3 replicate classifications and majority-vote aggregation (Fleiss' κ = `r round(feature_category_kappa, 3)`). Thus, the disclosure-category predictors in the regression were counts of newly disclosed vignette features by disclosure category, not manual labels assigned during simulation. The final combined model was estimated with OLS and cluster-robust standard errors at the conversation level.

To characterize how successive triage dispositions were formed, we additionally fit a belief-updating model in which the predicted triage disposition at turn t+1 was regressed on the model's predicted triage disposition at turn t, the model's own single-turn full-disclosure triage disposition, and the counts of newly disclosed features in each of the six disclosure categories, with each predictor interacted with turn.

Formally, writing $L_{m,i,t}$ for the triage disposition (A-D coded 0-3) that chatbot $m$ assigned to vignette $i$ at turn $t$, $O_{m,i}$ for that chatbot's single-turn full-disclosure disposition, and $d^{(c)}_{m,i,t}$ for the count of features of disclosure category $c$ newly disclosed at turn $t$, the model was

$$L_{m,i,t+1} = \alpha_m + \beta\, t + \big(w_a + w_a'\, t\big)\,L_{m,i,t} + \big(w_o + w_o'\, t\big)\,O_{m,i} + \sum_{c=1}^{6}\big(\gamma_c + \gamma_c'\, t\big)\,d^{(c)}_{m,i,t} + u_i + \varepsilon_{m,i,t},$$

with chatbot fixed intercepts $\alpha_m$, a vignette random intercept $u_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2_v)$, and residual $\varepsilon$. 

The turn-dependent weight on the chatbot's own running estimate, $w_a(t) = w_a + w_a'\,t$, quantifies the self-consistency bias: a positive $w_a'$ means later dispositions increasingly repeat the preceding-turn disposition, whereas $w_o(t) = w_o + w_o'\,t$ is the competing pull toward the chatbot's own full-disclosure judgment (`lmer(triage_t1 ~ model + triage_t*turn + oneshot*turn + newly_disclosed_by_category*turn + (1 | vignette))`). 

The model was estimated by restricted maximum likelihood (`lmer(triage_t1 ~ model + triage_t*turn + oneshot*turn + newly_disclosed_by_category*turn + (1 | vignette))`; lme4 and lmerTest), with Satterthwaite degrees of freedom for fixed-effect tests. The weight placed on the model's own prior triage disposition and on the single-turn triage disposition at a given turn were obtained from the corresponding coefficients and their turn interactions, with confidence intervals propagated from the fixed-effect covariance matrix. 

For each target chatbot, a steady-state disposition $L^*_m$ was obtained as the fixed point of the implied first-order dynamics, setting $L_{m,i,t+1}=L_{m,i,t}=L^*_m$ at the mean turn $\bar t$, the chatbot's mean single-turn judgment $\bar O$, and no new disclosure, which gives $L^*_m = \big(\alpha_m + \beta\,\bar t + w_o(\bar t)\,\bar O\big)\,/\,\big(1 - w_a(\bar t)\big)$. The steady-state signed error was then $e^*_m = L^*_m - \bar G$, where $\bar G$ is the mean benchmark level, so that positive values denote a net over-triage equilibrium.

## Clinician next-question comparison

Finally, we compared AI questions with clinician-authored counterfactual questions at intervention points. An intervention point is the turn at which a chatbot asked a question immediately before its predicted triage disposition changed. At each intervention point, the clinician-authored question replaced the chatbot's question at that same turn, before the disposition changed. We sampled `r n_human_moments` such intervention points from turns `r n_human_turn_min` through `r n_human_turn_max`, spanning models, vignettes, presentation clusters, and directions of triage change.

For each counterfactual, clinician raters saw the same truncated transcript that the AI question model had seen immediately before asking its actual next question, without the hidden vignette, benchmark label, future answer, or subsequent triage change. Clinicians wrote the next question they would ask at that point. This yielded `r n_human_compare` clinician-authored next-question annotations from `r n_human_raters` raters. Human and AI questions were then coded into the same 15-code taxonomy, enabling direct comparison of question focus and code overlap. We summarized agreement as the exact-match rate, the fraction of intervention points at which the clinician-authored and AI questions were assigned identical question codes. Using the same intervention points, we also measured, for each chatbot's actual next question and for the clinician-authored next questions, whether the question returned to a question category raised in the immediately preceding turn, and compared per-chatbot repetition rates with a paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test.

Clinician-vs-AI question differences were then summarized as code prevalence at the same sampled transcript states, with separate estimates for clinician-authored and AI-authored questions.

# Glossary

```{r}
#| label: glossary_table
#| echo: false
glossary_table <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Term, ~Meaning,
  "Benchmark triage label", "The original prespecified A-D triage label used to construct each vignette.",
  "Clinician clear-consensus label", "The independent clinician label used when at least 75% of clinician ratings selected the same A-D triage label.",
  "Target chatbot", "The model being evaluated for triage and next-question generation.",
  "Simulated user", "The model-generated user or collateral informant that answered questions using the hidden vignette as source material.",
  "Visible transcript", "The conversation content available to the target chatbot at a given turn.",
  "Triage-discriminative feature", "A vignette fact needed to distinguish the intended triage label from adjacent labels.",
  "Newly disclosed feature", "A triage-discriminative feature that became visible in the simulated user's latest message.",
  "Question code", "One of the 15 question categories used to classify the target chatbot's follow-up questions.",
  "Triage revision", "The change in the target chatbot's predicted triage disposition from one turn to the next.",
  "Under-triage", "A predicted triage disposition lower than the benchmark or clinician label.",
  "Over-triage", "A predicted triage disposition higher than the benchmark or clinician label.",
  "Intervention point", "The turn at which a chatbot's question was followed by an upward or downward change in its next triage disposition, and at which a counterfactual clinician-authored question was substituted."
)

knitr::kable(
  glossary_table,
  booktabs = TRUE,
  align = c("l", "l")
)
```

**Glossary.** Key terms used throughout the manuscript and supplement.

# Supplement

## Table S1

```{r}
#| label: table_s1_vignette_design
#| echo: false
knitr::kable(
  vignette_design_table,
  format = "pipe",
  booktabs = TRUE,
  align = c("l", "l", "l")

```

**Table S1.** Vignette design overview. The upper section summarizes the psychiatric presentation clusters represented in the corpus, what each cluster means clinically, and the focal risk dimensions paired with it. The lower section summarizes the focal risk dimensions, what each dimension means clinically, and the psychiatric presentation clusters paired with it.

## Table S2

```{r}
#| label: table_s2_vignette_fields
#| echo: false
knitr::kable(
  vignette_field_table,
  booktabs = TRUE,
  align = c("l", "l", "l")
)
```

**Table S2.** Vignette source fields used in the conversational psychiatric triage benchmark. Except for the FANG clinician consensus fields, vignette content fields were generated by Claude Opus 4.6 from clinician-authored design axes and prespecified benchmark labels. The clinical rationale is AI-generated, not a human clinician rating. Target chatbots saw only the visible conversation; the simulated user and analysis pipeline used the hidden fields.

## Table S3

```{r}
#| label: table_s3_prompt_inventory
#| echo: false
repo_root <- normalizePath("..", winslash = "/", mustWork = TRUE)

read_prompt_material <- function(path, source_type) {
  full_path <- file.path(repo_root, path)
  text <- paste(readLines(full_path, warn = FALSE), collapse = "\n")
  if (identical(source_type, "embedded_system_prompt")) {
    prompt_match <- str_match(text, 'SYSTEM_PROMPT\\s*=\\s*"""([\\s\\S]*?)"""')
    text <- ifelse(is.na(prompt_match[, 2]), text, prompt_match[, 2])
  }
  text <- str_replace_all(text, "`", "")
  str_replace_all(paste0("Source file: ", path, "\n\n", text), "\n", "<br>")
}

prompt_table <- prompt_inventory %>%
  mutate(
    `Exact text from file` = map2_chr(source_file, source_type, read_prompt_material)
  ) %>%
  select(`Prompt or material`, `Exact text from file`)

knitr::kable(
  prompt_table,
  format = "pipe",
  booktabs = TRUE,
  align = c("l", "l")
)
```

**Table S3.** Exact prompt materials for vignette generation, conversational simulation, target-chatbot triage and questioning, question classification, disclosure scoring, and classification of disclosed information.

## Table S4

```{r}
#| label: table_s4_turn_sequence
#| echo: false
turn_sequence_table <- tibble(
  `Stage` = c(
    "Opening",
    "Triage disposition at turn t",
    "Next-question selection at turn t",
    "Simulated-user reply",
    "Next turn"
  ),
  `Visible input` = c(
    "No visible transcript; the simulated user sees the hidden vignette.",
    "Visible transcript through the previous user message.",
    "The same visible transcript through the previous user message.",
    "Visible transcript plus the target chatbot's latest question.",
    "Visible transcript including the newly disclosed user reply."
  ),
  `Output` = c(
    "Broad naturalistic user message.",
    "Structured triage disposition, rationale, risk summary, next-step recommendation, and confidence.",
    "Exactly one follow-up question.",
    "Naturalistic answer constrained by the hidden vignette facts.",
    "Updated transcript for the next triage disposition."
  ),
  `Information not visible` = c(
    "Target chatbot does not see hidden facts or gold label.",
    "No hidden facts, gold label, future question, or prior triage outputs.",
    "No hidden facts, gold label, or prior triage outputs.",
    "No invented facts beyond the source vignette.",
    "The next triage disposition still sees only revealed information."
  )
)

knitr::kable(
  turn_sequence_table,
  booktabs = TRUE,
  align = c("l", "l", "l", "l")
)
```

**Table S4.** Order of events within the conversational simulation. At each turn, triage and next-question generation were separate API calls from the same visible transcript. A question at turn t could affect triage only after the simulated user answered it.

## Table S5

```{r}
#| label: table_s5_model_performance
#| echo: false
knitr::kable(
  final_turn_table,
  booktabs = TRUE,
  align = c("l", "l", rep("c", ncol(final_turn_table) - 2))
)
```

**Table S5.** Turn-resolved macro accuracy and triage error by target chatbot, shown at turns 1, 5, and `r max_turn` (early, mid, and final conversation). Turn 1 denotes the initial judgment from the opening message; turn `r max_turn` denotes the final judgment after `r max_turn` question-answer exchanges. Positive triage error indicates over-triage and negative triage error under-triage.

## Table S7

```{r}
#| label: table_s7_vignette_workflow
#| echo: false
vignette_workflow_table <- tibble::tribble(
  ~`Stage`, ~`What happens`, ~`Role in the benchmark`, ~`Example interpretation`,
  "Clinical design axes",
  "The vignette family was seeded by a presentation cluster, a focal risk dimension, and an intended A-D triage label.",
  "Defines the clinical space that the benchmark samples before any free-text case is written.",
  "A case family might be anchored on depression, self-neglect, and urgent-but-not-emergency care.",
  "Structured hidden case file",
  "Claude Opus 4.6 generated a complete third-person case file from those axes, including demographics, presenting problem, symptoms, focal risk facts, protective factors, relevant negatives, context, and an AI-generated rationale.",
  "This is the hidden ground-truth case. It is available to the simulated user and analysis pipeline, but never directly to the target chatbot.",
  "The hidden case could specify that the person is eating very little, losing weight, missing medication, and socially isolated, while also denying active suicidal intent.",
  "Discriminative case summary",
  "A second generation step compressed the structured case into the facts needed to distinguish the target triage label from adjacent labels.",
  "Turns the broad case file into a boundary-focused ground-truth summary: why this case belongs at this triage label rather than the neighboring labels.",
  "The summary would emphasize why worsening self-care requires prompt assessment, while also noting why there is no immediate emergency signal.",
  "Triage-discriminative checklist",
  "The discriminative-summary pipeline also listed the individual facts it relied on to make that boundary judgment.",
  "Defines the fact-level ground truth used later to ask whether the conversation uncovered the information that matters for triage.",
  "For the example case, triage-discriminative checklist items might include poor intake, weight loss, missed medication, absence of active suicidal intent, and whether anyone can check on the person.",
  "Clinician consensus annotation",
  "Registered medical doctors independently rated the appropriate A-D triage label. Consensus rules converted those ratings into clear, edge, or unclear clinician labels.",
  "Provides an independent human label source against which target-chatbot triage can be re-evaluated.",
  "If clinicians mostly choose the same level, the case has a clear clinician label; if ratings cluster across adjacent levels, it becomes an edge case.",
  "Conversational simulation",
  "The simulated user model received the hidden case and produced a broad opening message plus later answers constrained by the hidden facts. The target chatbot saw only the visible transcript.",
  "Transforms the static ground-truth case into a partial-information conversation.",
  "The opening might say only, 'I'm worried they are not looking after themselves.' If the target asks about eating, the simulated caller can then reveal poor intake.",
  "Disclosure scoring",
  "Claude Sonnet 4.6 judged, at each turn, whether each item in the triage-discriminative checklist had been disclosed in the cumulative user/caller transcript. The assistant's questions did not count as disclosure. Judgments used 3 replicates, majority vote, and monotonicity correction.",
  "Measures which triage-discriminative checklist items have become visible to the target chatbot over time.",
  "After an eating question, the triage-discriminative checklist item about poor intake may become disclosed; the item about missed medication may remain undisclosed until a later medication question.",
  "Disclosure category classification",
  "Claude Sonnet 4.6 separately classified each item in the triage-discriminative checklist by disclosure category: risk factor, protective factor, relevant negative, precipitating/maintaining factor, predisposing factor, or clinical symptom.",
  "Allows the analysis to distinguish whether newly visible triage-discriminative checklist items are risk-increasing, risk-reducing, symptom-related, or diagnostically important by their absence.",
  "A triage-discriminative checklist item about poor intake or weight loss may be a clinical symptom or risk factor; denial of suicidal intent is a relevant negative; reliable supervision is a protective factor.",
  "Triage-revision analysis",
  "Turn-level triage dispositions, question types, disclosure status for triage-discriminative checklist items, and disclosure categories were joined so that question/disclosure at turn t predicted the change from triage t to triage t+1.",
  "Links model questioning and newly revealed triage-discriminative checklist items to subsequent upward or downward triage revision.",
  "If the triage-discriminative checklist item about poor intake is disclosed after a question and the next triage disposition moves upward, that update is aligned with the newly visible clinical information."
)

knitr::kable(
  vignette_workflow_table,
  booktabs = TRUE,
  align = c("l", "l", "l", "l")
)
```

**Table S7.** Vignette construction and process measures. The table describes how a clinician-defined design cell is transformed into a hidden ground-truth case, a partial-information conversation, and turn-level process measures. The triage-discriminative checklist is created during vignette generation. Disclosure scoring asks whether each checklist item has become visible in the conversation, whereas disclosure-category classification labels which disclosure category that already-defined item belongs to.

```{r}
#| echo: false

  disclosure_category_table,
  booktabs = TRUE,
  align = c("l", "l")
)
```

**Table S8.** Disclosure categories used to classify newly disclosed triage-relevant vignette facts.

## Figure S1

```{r}
#| label: figure_s1_clinician_labels_data
#| include: false
fig_s1_never_disclosed <- final_fact_never_by_original_label %>%
  mutate(gold_triage = factor(gold_triage, levels = rev(triage_levels))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(y = gold_triage, x = proportion_never_disclosed, color = gold_triage)) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = 0, xend = proportion_never_disclosed, yend = gold_triage),
    linewidth = 1.0,
    alpha = 0.55
  ) +
  geom_point(size = manuscript_point_size * 1.25) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    limits = c(0, max(final_fact_never_by_original_label$proportion_never_disclosed, na.rm = TRUE) * 1.08),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.02))
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = triage_assumption_palette, guide = "none") +
  labs(
    x = "Checklist facts never disclosed by any target chatbot",
    y = "Benchmark triage",
    title = "D",
    subtitle = "Triage-discriminative facts never disclosed by the final scored turn"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

clinician_confusion_by_model_turn <- fang_clear_df %>%
  filter(!is.na(predicted_triage), !is.na(gold_triage)) %>%
  count(display_model, turn, gold_triage, predicted_triage, name = "n") %>%
  complete(
    display_model,
    turn = seq_len(max_turn),
    gold_triage = factor(triage_levels, levels = triage_levels),
    predicted_triage = factor(triage_levels, levels = triage_levels),
    fill = list(n = 0L)
  ) %>%
  group_by(display_model, turn, gold_triage) %>%
  mutate(prop = if (sum(n) > 0) n / sum(n) else NA_real_) %>%
  ungroup()

clinician_confusion_average_turn <- clinician_confusion_by_model_turn %>%
  group_by(turn, gold_triage, predicted_triage) %>%
  summarise(prop = mean(prop, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(
    turn_label = factor(paste0("Turn ", turn), levels = paste0("Turn ", seq_len(max_turn))),
    cell_direction = purrr::map2_chr(gold_triage, predicted_triage, classify_confusion_cell),
    fill_color = purrr::map2_chr(
      cell_direction,
      prop,
      ~ blend_to_white(triage_direction_palette[[.x]], pmin(pmax(.y, 0), 1))
    ),
    text_color = contrast_text_color(fill_color)
  )

fig_s3a <- ggplot(clinician_confusion_average_turn, aes(x = predicted_triage, y = gold_triage, fill = fill_color)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.35) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.0f", 100 * prop), color = text_color), size = heatmap_label_size) +
  facet_wrap(~ turn_label, nrow = 1) +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = rev(triage_levels)) +
  scale_fill_identity() +
  scale_color_identity(guide = "none") +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(x = "Predicted triage", y = "Clinician triage", title = "A", subtitle = "Average confusion matrices by turn") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    strip.placement = "outside",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

clinician_disclosure_accuracy_turn_model <- disclosure_by_fang_level_turn %>%
  mutate(turn = turn + 1L) %>%
  inner_join(
    fang_by_gold_turn %>%
      select(display_model, gold_triage, turn, accuracy, signed_error),
    by = c("display_model", "fang_clear_triage" = "gold_triage", "turn")
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    turn_factor = factor(turn, levels = seq_len(max_turn), labels = paste0("Turn ", seq_len(max_turn))),
    gold_triage = factor(as.character(fang_clear_triage), levels = triage_levels)
  )

clinician_disclosure_accuracy_turn_average <- clinician_disclosure_accuracy_turn_model %>%
  group_by(gold_triage, turn, turn_factor) %>%
  summarise(
    accuracy = mean(accuracy, na.rm = TRUE),
    signed_error = mean(signed_error, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_disclosure = mean(mean_disclosure, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

clinician_figure_s2_turn_alpha_values <- scales::rescale(seq_len(max_turn), to = c(0.08, 1), from = c(1, max_turn))
names(clinician_figure_s2_turn_alpha_values) <- paste0("Turn ", seq_len(max_turn))

clinician_disclosure_accuracy_turn_model <- clinician_disclosure_accuracy_turn_model %>%
  mutate(
    turn_alpha = unname(clinician_figure_s2_turn_alpha_values[as.character(turn_factor)]),
    point_fill = scales::alpha(model_palette[as.character(display_model)], turn_alpha)
  )

clinician_disclosure_accuracy_turn_average <- clinician_disclosure_accuracy_turn_average %>%
  mutate(
    turn_alpha = unname(clinician_figure_s2_turn_alpha_values[as.character(turn_factor)]),
    point_fill = scales::alpha("black", turn_alpha)
  )

plot_clinician_disclosure_metric <- function(metric, y_label, title, subtitle = NULL, y_limits = NULL, y_breaks = waiver(), y_percent = FALSE) {
  if (is.null(y_limits)) {
    y_limits <- metric_limits(
      c(clinician_disclosure_accuracy_turn_model[[metric]], clinician_disclosure_accuracy_turn_average[[metric]]),
      include_zero = TRUE
    )
  }
  reference_layer <- if (metric %in% c("accuracy", "signed_error")) {
    geom_hline(
      data = clinician_full_vignette_by_gold_average,
      aes(yintercept = .data[[metric]]),
      linetype = "dashed",
      color = "grey45",
      linewidth = 0.32
    )
  } else {
    NULL
  }
  ggplot(
    clinician_disclosure_accuracy_turn_model,
    aes(x = mean_disclosure, y = .data[[metric]], color = display_model, group = display_model)
  ) +
    reference_layer +
    geom_path(linewidth = 0.42, alpha = 0.45, lineend = "round") +
    geom_point(
      aes(fill = point_fill),
      shape = 21,
      size = manuscript_point_size * 0.90,
      stroke = 0.65,
      color = "black"
    ) +
    geom_path(
      data = clinician_disclosure_accuracy_turn_average,
      aes(x = mean_disclosure, y = .data[[metric]], group = gold_triage),
      inherit.aes = FALSE,
      color = "black",
      linewidth = 1.05,
      alpha = 0.92,
      lineend = "round"
    ) +
    geom_point(
      data = clinician_disclosure_accuracy_turn_average,
      aes(x = mean_disclosure, y = .data[[metric]], fill = point_fill),
      inherit.aes = FALSE,
      shape = 21,
      color = "black",
      size = manuscript_point_size * 0.90,
      stroke = 0.75
    ) +
    facet_grid(. ~ gold_triage, labeller = as_labeller(triage_labels_short)) +
    scale_color_manual(values = model_palette, name = NULL, drop = FALSE) +
    scale_fill_identity() +
    scale_x_continuous(
      labels = label_percent(accuracy = 1),
      limits = c(0, 1),
      breaks = c(0, 0.5, 1)
    ) +
    scale_y_continuous(
      labels = if (y_percent) label_percent(accuracy = 1) else waiver(),
      limits = y_limits,
      breaks = y_breaks
    ) +
    labs(
      x = "Discriminative features disclosed",
      y = y_label,
      title = title,
      subtitle = subtitle
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
    theme(
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      legend.position = "none",
      strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.95)
    ) +
    guides(color = "none", fill = "none")
}

primary_metric_model <- fang_by_gold_turn %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(accuracy, signed_error, absolute_error),
    names_to = "metric",
    values_to = "value"
  )

primary_metric_average <- primary_metric_model %>%
  group_by(gold_triage, turn, metric) %>%
  summarise(value = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

primary_metric_limits <- primary_metric_model %>%
  bind_rows(primary_metric_average) %>%
  group_by(metric) %>%
  summarise(
    lower = case_when(
      first(metric) == "accuracy" ~ metric_limits(value, lower_bound = 0, upper_bound = 1)[1],
      first(metric) == "signed_error" ~ metric_limits(value, lower_bound = -3, upper_bound = 3, include_zero = TRUE)[1],
      first(metric) == "absolute_error" ~ metric_limits(value, lower_bound = 0, upper_bound = 3, include_zero = TRUE)[1]
    ),
    upper = case_when(
      first(metric) == "accuracy" ~ metric_limits(value, lower_bound = 0, upper_bound = 1)[2],
      first(metric) == "signed_error" ~ metric_limits(value, lower_bound = -3, upper_bound = 3, include_zero = TRUE)[2],
      first(metric) == "absolute_error" ~ metric_limits(value, lower_bound = 0, upper_bound = 3, include_zero = TRUE)[2]
    ),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

get_clinician_metric_limits <- function(metric_name) {
  limits <- primary_metric_limits %>% filter(metric == metric_name)
  c(limits$lower[1], limits$upper[1])
}

plot_clinician_primary_metric <- function(metric_name, title, subtitle, y_label, percent = FALSE, show_legend = FALSE) {
  df_model <- primary_metric_model %>% filter(metric == metric_name, is.finite(value))
  df_avg <- primary_metric_average %>% filter(metric == metric_name, is.finite(value))
  y_limits <- get_clinician_metric_limits(metric_name)
  y_breaks <- metric_breaks(y_limits, percent = percent)
  y_scale <- if (percent) {
    scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent(accuracy = 1), limits = y_limits, breaks = y_breaks)
  } else {
    scale_y_continuous(limits = y_limits, breaks = y_breaks)
  }
  model_guide <- if (show_legend) {
    guide_legend(nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE, override.aes = list(linewidth = 0.6, alpha = 1, size = manuscript_point_size * 0.85))
  } else {
    "none"
  }

  p <- ggplot()
  if (metric_name == "accuracy") {
    p <- p +
      geom_hline(
        data = clinician_full_vignette_by_gold_average,
        aes(yintercept = accuracy),
        linetype = "dashed",
        color = "grey45",
        linewidth = 0.32
      )
  }
  if (metric_name == "signed_error") {
    p <- p +
      geom_hline(
        data = clinician_full_vignette_by_gold_average,
        aes(yintercept = signed_error),
        linetype = "dashed",
        color = "grey45",
        linewidth = 0.32
      )
  }
  p +
    geom_line(
      data = df_model,
      aes(x = turn, y = value, color = display_model, group = display_model),
      linewidth = 0.35,
      alpha = 0.55
    ) +
    geom_point(
      data = df_model,
      aes(x = turn, y = value, fill = display_model),
      shape = 21,
      color = "black",
      size = manuscript_point_size * 0.90,
      stroke = 0.65,
      alpha = 0.65
    ) +
    geom_line(
      data = df_avg,
      aes(x = turn, y = value, group = 1),
      color = "black",
      linewidth = 0.95
    ) +
    geom_point(
      data = df_avg,
      aes(x = turn, y = value),
      color = "black",
      size = manuscript_point_size + 0.2
    ) +
    facet_grid(. ~ gold_triage, labeller = as_labeller(triage_labels_short)) +
    scale_color_manual(values = model_palette, name = "Target chatbot", guide = model_guide) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = model_palette, name = "Target chatbot", guide = "none") +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq_len(max_turn)) +
    y_scale +
    labs(x = "Turn", y = y_label, title = title, subtitle = subtitle) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
    theme(
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.95),
      legend.position = "bottom",
      legend.title = element_blank(),
      legend.text = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.68),
      legend.key.width = grid::unit(0.75, "lines"),
      legend.key.height = grid::unit(0.45, "lines"),
      legend.spacing.x = grid::unit(0.08, "lines"),
      legend.spacing.y = grid::unit(0.02, "lines")
    )
}

fig_s2b_accuracy <- plot_clinician_disclosure_metric(
  "accuracy",
  "Exact-match accuracy",
  "B",
  "Accuracy as discriminative information becomes visible",
  y_limits = c(0, 1),
  y_breaks = c(0, 0.5, 1),
  y_percent = TRUE
)

fig_s2b_signed <- plot_clinician_disclosure_metric(
  "signed_error",
  "Mean triage error",
  "B",
  "Triage error as discriminative information becomes visible",
  y_limits = c(-2, 1),
  y_breaks = -2:1,
  y_percent = FALSE
)

# Clinician-label statistics reported in the Figure S1 caption (mirror of the
# benchmark-label statistics reported in the main text for Figure 2).
fang_by_model_turn <- fang_by_gold_turn %>%
  group_by(display_model, turn) %>%
  summarise(
    accuracy = mean(accuracy, na.rm = TRUE),
    signed_error = mean(signed_error, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
clinician_turn_accuracy_overall <- turn_paired_comparison(fang_by_model_turn, "accuracy")
clinician_turn_signed_error_A <- turn_paired_comparison(fang_by_gold_turn %>% filter(gold_triage == "A"), "signed_error")
clinician_turn_signed_error_B <- turn_paired_comparison(fang_by_gold_turn %>% filter(gold_triage == "B"), "signed_error")
clinician_turn_signed_error_C <- turn_paired_comparison(fang_by_gold_turn %>% filter(gold_triage == "C"), "signed_error")
clinician_turn_signed_error_D <- turn_paired_comparison(fang_by_gold_turn %>% filter(gold_triage == "D"), "signed_error")
clinician_turn_accuracy_overall_text <- fmt_t_test(clinician_turn_accuracy_overall)
clinician_turn_signed_error_A_text <- fmt_t_test(clinician_turn_signed_error_A)
clinician_turn_signed_error_B_text <- fmt_t_test(clinician_turn_signed_error_B)
clinician_turn_signed_error_C_text <- fmt_t_test(clinician_turn_signed_error_C)
clinician_turn_signed_error_D_text <- fmt_t_test(clinician_turn_signed_error_D)
clinician_A_turn1_signed <- turn_metric(fang_by_gold_turn, "A", 1, "signed_error")
clinician_A_final_signed <- turn_metric(fang_by_gold_turn, "A", max_turn, "signed_error")
clinician_B_turn1_signed <- turn_metric(fang_by_gold_turn, "B", 1, "signed_error")
clinician_B_final_signed <- turn_metric(fang_by_gold_turn, "B", max_turn, "signed_error")
clinician_C_turn1_signed <- turn_metric(fang_by_gold_turn, "C", 1, "signed_error")
clinician_C_final_signed <- turn_metric(fang_by_gold_turn, "C", max_turn, "signed_error")
clinician_D_turn1_signed <- turn_metric(fang_by_gold_turn, "D", 1, "signed_error")
clinician_D_final_signed <- turn_metric(fang_by_gold_turn, "D", max_turn, "signed_error")
clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_spearman <- suppressWarnings(cor.test(
  clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_error$full_disclosure_signed_error,
  clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_error$final_conversation_signed_error,
  method = "spearman",
  exact = FALSE
))
clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_R <- sprintf("%.2f", unname(clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_spearman$estimate))
clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_P <- fmt_p(clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_spearman$p.value)

fig_s2c_signed_compare <- ggplot(
  clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_error,
  aes(x = full_disclosure_signed_error, y = final_conversation_signed_error, fill = display_model)
) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey65", linewidth = 0.35) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dotted", color = "grey70", linewidth = 0.25) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dotted", color = "grey70", linewidth = 0.25) +
  geom_point(
    shape = 21,
    color = "black",
    size = manuscript_point_size * 0.90,
    stroke = 0.65,
    alpha = 0.85
  ) +
  geom_segment(
    data = clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_centroid,
    aes(x = full_disclosure_signed_error, xend = full_disclosure_signed_error, y = clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_limits[1], yend = final_conversation_signed_error),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    color = "black",
    linetype = "dashed",
    linewidth = 0.95,
    alpha = 1
  ) +
  geom_segment(
    data = clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_centroid,
    aes(x = clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_limits[1], xend = full_disclosure_signed_error, y = final_conversation_signed_error, yend = final_conversation_signed_error),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    color = "black",
    linetype = "dashed",
    linewidth = 0.95,
    alpha = 1
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_centroid,
    aes(x = full_disclosure_signed_error, y = final_conversation_signed_error),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    color = "black",
    size = manuscript_point_size * 1.15,
    alpha = 0.95
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data = clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_facet_text,
    aes(x = x, y = y, label = label),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    hjust = -0.02,
    vjust = 1.2,
    size = base_font_size * 0.24
  ) +
  facet_grid(. ~ gold_triage, labeller = as_labeller(triage_labels_short)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = model_palette, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_limits) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_limits) +
  labs(
    x = "Full-disclosure single-turn triage error",
    y = "Final conversational triage error",
    title = "C",
    subtitle = "Triage errors at the final turn of a conversation vs one-shot triage errors"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none",
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_font_size * 0.72),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )
figure_s2_model_legend_plot <- ggplot(
  clinician_disclosure_accuracy_turn_model %>% distinct(display_model, mean_disclosure, accuracy),
  aes(x = mean_disclosure, y = accuracy, color = display_model)
) +
  geom_line(aes(group = display_model), linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = model_palette, name = NULL, drop = FALSE) +
  theme_void(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.text = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.62),
    legend.key.height = unit(0.24, "cm"),
    legend.key.width = unit(0.42, "cm")
  ) +
  guides(color = guide_legend(nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE))

figure_s2_model_legend <- extract_legend(figure_s2_model_legend_plot)
```

```{r}
#| label: figure_s1_clinician_labels
#| echo: false
#| out-width: 100%
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 8.5
fig_s1c_accuracy <- ggplot(
  label_source_turn_accuracy_wide,
  aes(x = original_accuracy, y = clinician_accuracy, fill = display_model)
) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey65", linewidth = 0.35) +
  geom_point(shape = 21, color = "black", size = manuscript_point_size * 0.90, stroke = 0.65, alpha = 0.85) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 0.03,
    y = 0.97,
    label = label_source_turn_accuracy_spearman_text,
    hjust = 0,
    vjust = 1,
    size = base_font_size * 0.30
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = model_palette, name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_percent(accuracy = 1), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent(accuracy = 1), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(
    x = "Accuracy under original benchmark labels",
    y = "Accuracy under clinician consensus labels",
    title = "C",
    subtitle = "Accuracy agreement across benchmark and clinician labels"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +

    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),

  )

figure_s1_row2 <- gridExtra::arrangeGrob(
  fig_s2b_signed,
  fig_s1c_accuracy,

gridExtra::grid.arrange(
  fig_s3a,
  figure_s1_row2,
  figure_s2_model_legend,

)
```

**Figure S1.** Conversational triage performance under clinician clear-consensus labels. **A.** Average confusion matrices across target chatbots at each conversational turn (turns 1–`r max_turn`, arranged left-to-right); each row sums to 100%. Blue cells indicate correct predictions, green over-triage, and red under-triage relative to the clinician clear-consensus label. Under clinician clear-consensus labels, accuracy rose from `r fmt_pct(fang_turn1_acc)` at turn 1 to `r fmt_pct(fang_final_acc)` at turn `r max_turn` (paired t-test across models: `r clinician_turn_accuracy_overall_text`). **B.** Mean triage error as triage-discriminative information is disclosed over the conversation, shown separately for clinician consensus levels A–D. Colored lines represent individual chatbots and black lines their average; lighter points are earlier turns. The dashed horizontal line shows average performance when the complete vignette was presented in one message. Positive values indicate over-triage and negative values under-triage. Triage errors shifted upward across turns (A: from `r sprintf("%+.2f", clinician_A_turn1_signed)` to `r sprintf("%+.2f", clinician_A_final_signed)`, `r clinician_turn_signed_error_A_text`; B: from `r sprintf("%+.2f", clinician_B_turn1_signed)` to `r sprintf("%+.2f", clinician_B_final_signed)`, `r clinician_turn_signed_error_B_text`; C: from `r sprintf("%+.2f", clinician_C_turn1_signed)` to `r sprintf("%+.2f", clinician_C_final_signed)`, `r clinician_turn_signed_error_C_text`; D: from `r sprintf("%+.2f", clinician_D_turn1_signed)` to `r sprintf("%+.2f", clinician_D_final_signed)`, `r clinician_turn_signed_error_D_text`). **C.** Paired model-by-turn accuracy under original benchmark labels versus clinician clear-consensus labels, restricted to vignettes with clear clinician consensus so that both label sources summarize the same case subset; each point represents one target chatbot at one conversational turn, and the dashed line denotes equality between label sources. Final-turn conversational signed error correlated with single-turn full-disclosure error on the same cases (Spearman R = `r clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_R`, P `r clinician_conversation_full_disclosure_signed_P`).


## Figure S2

```{r}
#| label: figure_s2_disclosure_overview_data
#| include: false

# Panel A: information disclosure by conversational turn (per chatbot and average).
disclosure_turn_model_overall <- disclosure_analysis_df %>%
  group_by(display_model, turn) %>%
  summarise(mean_disclosure = mean(proportion_disclosed, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(turn = turn + 1L)
disclosure_turn_overall_summary <- disclosure_turn_model_overall %>%
  group_by(turn) %>%
  summarise(mean_ci_95(mean_disclosure), .groups = "drop")

fig_s13b <- ggplot(disclosure_turn_model_overall, aes(x = turn, y = mean_disclosure)) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = one_shot_disclosure_overall_mean,
    linetype = "dotted",
    color = "grey45",
    linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = max_turn, y = one_shot_disclosure_overall_mean,
    label = "single-turn ceiling",
    hjust = 1, vjust = -0.5,
    size = base_font_size * 0.22,
    color = "grey35"
  ) +
  geom_line(aes(color = display_model, group = display_model), linewidth = 0.35, alpha = 0.55) +
  geom_point(
    aes(fill = display_model),
    shape = 21,
    color = "black",
    size = manuscript_point_size * 0.85,
    stroke = 0.55,
    alpha = 0.6
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = disclosure_turn_overall_summary,
    aes(x = turn, y = mean, group = 1),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.95
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = disclosure_turn_overall_summary,
    aes(x = turn, y = mean),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    color = "black",
    size = manuscript_point_size
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = model_palette, guide = "none") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = model_palette, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq_len(max_turn)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent(accuracy = 1), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(
    x = "Turn",
    y = "Discriminative features disclosed",
    title = "A",
    subtitle = "Cumulative disclosure across turns"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

# Panel B: directed (signed) triage error vs disclosure at the vignette level. One
# point per vignette (averaged across chatbots), colored by benchmark urgency. A flat
# cloud indicates that how much was disclosed did not predict the direction of error.
# (disclosure_error_by_vignette and its correlation label are computed above, in the
# disclosure results chunk, so the same numbers appear in the text.)
fig_s13d <- ggplot(
  disclosure_error_by_vignette,
  aes(x = mean_disclosure, y = mean_signed_error)
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey55", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_point(
    aes(fill = gold_triage),
    shape = 21,
    color = "black",
    size = manuscript_point_size * 0.9,
    stroke = 0.65,
    alpha = 0.85
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = min(disclosure_error_by_vignette$mean_disclosure, na.rm = TRUE),
    y = max(disclosure_error_by_vignette$mean_signed_error, na.rm = TRUE),
    label = paste0(disclosure_error_vignette_cor_label, "\n(n = ", disclosure_error_vignette_n, " vignettes)"),
    hjust = 0, vjust = 1,
    size = base_font_size * 0.24
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = triage_assumption_palette, name = "Benchmark triage") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_percent(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous() +
  labs(
    x = "Mean discriminative features disclosed (final turn)",
    y = "Mean triage error",
    title = "B",
    subtitle = "Triage error vs disclosure (per vignette)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# Panel E: disclosure in the two triage settings (conversational final turn vs
# single-turn full-disclosure), summarised across chatbots by benchmark level. The
# single-turn message approximates a disclosure ceiling; the gap to the final
# conversational turn is triage-relevant information the conversation left unelicited.
conv_final_disclosure_by_model_level <- disclosure_analysis_df %>%
  filter(turn == disclosure_final_scored_turn, !is.na(gold_triage)) %>%
  group_by(display_model, gold_triage) %>%
  summarise(mean_disclosure = mean(proportion_disclosed, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(setting = "Conversational (final turn)")

one_shot_disclosure_by_model_level <- one_shot_disclosure_df %>%
  group_by(display_model, gold_triage) %>%
  summarise(mean_disclosure = mean(proportion_disclosed, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(setting = "single-turn (full disclosure)")

disclosure_two_settings_model <- bind_rows(
  conv_final_disclosure_by_model_level,
  one_shot_disclosure_by_model_level
) %>%
  mutate(setting = factor(setting, levels = c("Conversational (final turn)", "single-turn (full disclosure)")))

disclosure_two_settings_summary <- disclosure_two_settings_model %>%
  group_by(setting, gold_triage) %>%
  summarise(mean_ci_95(mean_disclosure), .groups = "drop")

disclosure_setting_shapes <- c(
  "Conversational (final turn)" = 21,
  "single-turn (full disclosure)" = 24
)

fig_s13e <- ggplot(
  disclosure_two_settings_model,
  aes(x = gold_triage, y = mean_disclosure)
) +
  geom_point(
    aes(fill = display_model, shape = setting, group = setting),
    position = position_jitterdodge(jitter.width = 0.14, dodge.width = 0.6, seed = 1),
    color = "black",
    size = manuscript_point_size * 0.9,
    stroke = 0.65,
    alpha = 0.85
  ) +
  geom_linerange(
    data = disclosure_two_settings_summary,
    aes(x = gold_triage, ymin = lower, ymax = upper, group = setting),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.6),
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = disclosure_two_settings_summary,
    aes(x = gold_triage, y = mean, shape = setting, group = setting),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.6),
    fill = "black",
    color = "black",
    size = manuscript_point_size * 1.05,
    stroke = 0.5
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = model_palette, guide = "none", drop = FALSE) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values = disclosure_setting_shapes,
    name = NULL,
    labels = c("Conversational (final turn)", "single-turn (full disclosure)")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent(accuracy = 1)) +
  guides(shape = guide_legend(override.aes = list(fill = "grey40", size = manuscript_point_size))) +
  labs(
    x = "Benchmark triage",
    y = "Discriminative features disclosed",
    title = "D",
    subtitle = "Disclosure in conversational vs single-turn triage"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# Panel E: does disclosing less in a conversation (relative to the single-turn message
# for the SAME case and chatbot) go with losing triage accuracy? Aggregated to one
# point per vignette (mean across chatbots), colored by benchmark urgency.
# (disclosure_gap_by_vignette and its correlation label are computed above, in the
# disclosure results chunk, so the same numbers appear in the text.)
fig_s13g <- ggplot(
  disclosure_gap_by_vignette,
  aes(x = mean_disclosure_gap, y = mean_accuracy_gap)
) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey70", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey70", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_point(
    aes(fill = gold_triage),
    shape = 21,
    color = "black",
    size = manuscript_point_size * 0.9,
    stroke = 0.65,
    alpha = 0.85
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = min(disclosure_gap_by_vignette$mean_disclosure_gap, na.rm = TRUE),
    y = 0.95,
    label = paste0(disclosure_gap_vignette_cor_label, "\n(n = ", disclosure_gap_vignette_n, " vignettes)"),
    hjust = 0, vjust = 1,
    size = base_font_size * 0.24
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = triage_assumption_palette, name = "Benchmark triage") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_percent(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-1, 1)) +
  labs(
    x = "Mean disclosure gap (single-turn \u2212 conversational)",
    y = "Mean accuracy gap (single-turn \u2212 conversational)",
    title = "E",
    subtitle = "Disclosure gap vs accuracy gap (per vignette)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: figure_s2_disclosure_overview
#| echo: false
#| out-width: 100%

#| fig-height: 13
figure_s13_row1 <- gridExtra::arrangeGrob(fig_s13b, fig_s13d, ncol = 2, widths = c(1, 1))
figure_s13_row3 <- gridExtra::arrangeGrob(fig_s13e, fig_s13g, ncol = 2, widths = c(1, 1))
gridExtra::grid.arrange(
```
